description: Optimization migration on a 4B-row telemetry table that uses UUID v4 as PK — review index/cluster strategy in light of PK choice
review_checklist: Evaluate UUID v4 vs ULID/UUID v7 for clustered/B-tree write patterns
Verify CLUSTER lock semantics on production-sized tables
Check fillfactor implications for write-heavy vs read-heavy tables
Identify root-cause optimization vs band-aid fixes

ground_truth.description: UUID v4 as PRIMARY KEY on a 4B-row write-heavy table is a fundamental architectural mistake. Random UUIDs cause every INSERT to write to a different B-tree leaf page, destroying buffer cache hit rate and forcing constant page splits. Cache-effective working set is O(table_size) instead of O(recent_data). Use ULID or UUID v7 (k-sortable) for an immediate ~5x write throughput improvement and 50%+ cache hit rate gain.
Composite index without CONCURRENTLY on 4B rows blocks all writes for the duration (estimated 8-15 hours). Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

fillfactor=100 on a write-heavy table prevents HOT (Heap-Only Tuple) updates. Every UPDATE will require a new index entry, doubling write amplification. For tables with frequent UPDATEs, fillfactor=80-90 leaves room for in-page tuple replacement. Combined with UUID v4 PK, this guarantees worst-case write performance.

CLUSTER acquires AccessExclusiveLock for the full duration — for a 4B-row table this is days of complete unavailability. CLUSTER is also one-time; INSERT/UPDATE traffic immediately undoes the ordering. For ordering benefits use pg_repack (online) or partition by time.

GIN index on JSONB tags column without specifying jsonb_path_ops doubles index size and slows updates. If queries only use containment (@>), `USING gin(tags jsonb_path_ops)` is dramatically smaller and faster.
